titleStmt.title: Morocco – Shawiya – Beni Mesnawi – Feasts (2) (2022)
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Morocco Fieldwork Campaign Terlan Djavadova 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: She told you, do you know…do you know each other, other than your tribe? Is there a connection with other tribes?

[…the ones that are around you.] The one that are our dearest. [Tell her, can you name me the tribes that you know here, around you? do you know their names, can you tell me the names of the tribes?] i know wlad Mha l-Kbir, I know Smaʕna, I know Kʷdia, I know Šwafʕa, I know l-Bazat, I know the l-Kʷdiya, that their name.
I know Fŭqra, I know the village Fqih, I know Šbibat, l-Bḥura, Wlad Sliman, the village Sidi ʕəllal, š-Šuggʷa...

Mllasa…and many regions that we know. [Is there any connection or do you know them only from far?] No, we know them from far, and there are between them our relatives, and between them our dearest. [Do you intermarry with each other?]
My husband’s sister, got married to someone from the Wlad Sidi Ben Dawud, my daughter, she is in Smaʕna.

The kid of this one here is with the Bazat … there is someone with Beni Meskin, far. There is … everyone where … regions. But there are some, where we go to express our condolescence with him, and we go and come with him, there are someone we just know. [And there are … you do not … only … some you do not get along with at all?]

[For example, you hear anything bad about them, and exclude them?] No! [No, that doesn’t exist, so you live good together?] Living together with us…with…with us…we do not have contact with them, but they are good with us, there is no one…so…

Rivals with him or … [so in this issue example when they come to engage with one of you, and you engage with one of them, you accept, there is no „no, I dont want one of that tribe“] no, … we accept, we accept they have to give only … each of them.
everyone says I want to work, (she) wants to learn (even if there is a discussion, just a simple dicussion…not that discussion] this one came from Zagoura, this one. [yes, you can see it]
there is a hostility against someone or someone is hostile against you. You keep talking, keep talking, it goes between us it’s not…

even if there is a discussion, the hearts are in peace, they come back quickly [and this is how the real Moroccan people were living, these are the original Moroccans, regarding the people of the city, they are bad] no, sister, no, we don’t interrupt […] between them, we cant, we cant [good, thank God]
Everything is peaceful as if the lam comes here, they come to the lamat.
[Lamat, what is this?] like they build l-fragat they slaughter cows. the fragat, as well what are they? ther just was a lama last Sunday.
Tell me, what are fragat or explain me little by little, please. you dont know a frag? a big frag …
Tents, tents ... [tents] they buy ... they slaughter a cow ... do you know what a cow is?
They buy a cow, they buy groceries, gravel, pumkin, vegetables, spices, olive oil, and oil.
All the vegetables, they bring a pot, of the people of the village. [Mramet what are mramet?] Mramet is a big pot. The girls from here gather, the women of the village cook.
They do qṣari, they bring singing dancers, they bring things like that…two maramit they do in it meat, and the pot they do in it a certain amount of meat and put kuskus on it…the big pots.
They steam, they do thirty qṣriya, thirty-five, and the men of the village gather and eat and drink tea, and tray and tea pots.
They brushed (their hair) and laughed, awaiting that Saturday. ??? they made a feast ... and two tents
One they just bought, one they bought, one was old like ### and they made a wedding for her, sometimes if they dont want kuskus, they make merqa.
And sfa and merqa and so on, sometimes they go to a saint, far in this direction…they slaughter there a cow and split the meat and bring it here to the village.
Each house cooks a pot of Kuskus (…) and they load trucks and go.

These days they dont do kuskus together anymore … [its better when they get together?] when they get together around one big pot and around single pots (for kuskus), and the food stays warm in this place. [The women of the village were backing bread in this oven].
They sent them bread (krun). What is krum? This bread. we call it l-krun, ist called krun….[You dont know why ist called krum?]

no, … we, when they build this oven, we start put it in, we call it krun. [but you dont know where the name came from, fine, complete please, sorry] we were sending them krun and they bring things like butter.
for marmelade and olive oil and the men are having fun and they make tea. [ka-ytiyyu thats making tea?] and they keep having fun…they as well invite their friends, some come from Casablanca.
All the ones who took part giving them money, they invite them to help them, they come and eat and drink with them.
(1754) and then they carry the frak and then they go into the mosque with it.
They make a special room for it, they put it in the mosque, when there is death or a wedding, they take it out with its dishes and glasses and everything that it has.

with tables and everything, thats what it is, sister. [I understand, thank you, and now this…you said lama, ist called lama, this lama you do it in a particular time like you count the weeks and months or it just happens?] No, from a year, every two years, three, then they make this lama, not always. Not always, I understand, thank you.]

[what…when a guest comes, what do you do for him? for example, do you have any obligatories, when a guest comes, were you say its we have to do this or do you only welcome him?] Just what God gave us, we put in front of him.
what we have more, we will put it in front of him, if we don’t have, he will eat what we have, …we buy the groceries for him, we buy the groceries, if he tells us he is coming …
Today we have a market, didn’t you pass by? [no, Thursday, we come from it.] We go by tractors or by taxi, we go buying groceries from it and we go.
This is, what God gave us, we bring it, this is what there is, and we bring it to him goods and if there is butter and if there is ???, we put it in front of him.
There is no…when there is nothing and we didn’t buy groceries or nothing, what God gave us he will eat with us.
This is what it is…when the taxi appeared, and if the taxi has to come until they come, and I stayed starring at it, I knew you are foreigners.
[I want to ask please, again…do you have any superstitions or any customs and traditions, that you practice? something that you must do? ]

[How, for example, you celebrate for example, I tell you an example, Aishura for example you have to, you do something or Aid kbir is obligatory to do something, you have here something special for you…tell me for example something what you do here, only you, that is different from otehr people.]
Like, you mean when we gather or what?

[as you like, anything you do, in a way…] Ayshura, at Ayshura they gather, like this, the neighbors make gdidda, they gather, each one bring a little of gdid from her, and bring a little bit of groceries, like smida, like things, like vegetables and so on, we gather, we gahter again ….the girls gahter and dance and sing and dance and chat and …from the qassem.
[qasam?] qasam is couscous, (this is couscous, say keskesku) …keskesku, the big eat it, the small eat, the ones who are sitting eat it…

Like one that left people at home, picks up and brings to her husband a little or her child a little and so on, they laugh and play and they have fun. The eid kbir, again they gather again like this…each one and everyday they gather at one person, and when the eid ends, and the guests go or your children go, or the ones who are at home and that you worry about go.
The neighbors gather like this, today I invited, calling everyone, the day after …the day after another woman, invites, calling all the neighbors and we make a round.

The circle between us, today we eat at this one’s, and tomorrow we eat at the other, tomorrow at the other, so that what we do at the eid kbir. [ist a must for you] ist amust, a must…at awashir, this is the one we gather at. [awashir, what is this] It is the ashura i told you about. Nnayir, this is we celebrate here. [What is this?] Nnayr. The new year.
When the first month comes…when the forst month comes, the other people, the ones formthe city celebrate new years eve, they make sweets and so on, we slaughter a rooster and make bread.

We put lentils and fava beans and so on and legumes on it and we cook it and we pour over and we decorate it with candies and eggs and we put the rooster on it and we put the sauce out of lentils and hummus and so on and we eat it.
